subject: Kelo v. New London (2005), eminent domain, and the meaning of 'public use'
central_question: The Constitution lets the government take your home for 'public use' if it pays you. But what if 'public use' means handing your home to a private developer — and you have done nothing wrong?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how a 2005 Supreme Court decision read two words in the Constitution — 'public use' — so broadly that a city could take well-kept homes and give the land to private development, and what happened next.
surprise: The government did not take these homes for a road or a school; it took them, intact and blameless, to hand to a private redevelopment plan — and the Supreme Court said that counts as 'public use.'
stakes: Whether the government can condemn your home, paying you market value, to transfer it to another private party in the name of jobs and tax revenue.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in law, property rights and how systems work
demand_evidence: 'The government can take your home and give it to a developer' is a famous, bipartisan-outrage, high-search story (Kelo is one of the most recognized modern property-rights cases).
Self-relevant property-rights angle with durable long-tail interest.
competition: Coverage is split between advocacy and news; few give a neutral 'what the Court actually held + why it backfired' explainer.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant framing (your home); neutral and primary-source-cited; the closing chapter of the 'what the state can TAKE' cluster (after the EP6-8 'what the state can SEE' trilogy and EP9 forfeiture).
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (545 U.S. 469); Cornell LII and Oyez provide the full record; the project's failure and the house relocation are well documented.
visual_feasibility: High: a small house standing alone amid cleared lots; the 'little pink house' motif; a 'public use' two-word zoom; a before/after of the vacant Fort Trumbull site.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2005); opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Real people (Susette Kelo and neighbors) described by role, neutrally and sympathetically-but-factually; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
Keep neutral and non-partisan: the backlash was bipartisan; present the city's economic-development rationale fairly alongside the owners' side.
State-reform count varies by source — use a range (~40-plus) and attribute (Institute for Justice); note many reforms were later criticized as weak.

Pfizer / New London Development Corporation are real entities — state only documented facts; NLDC was a private nonprofit acting as the city's development agent (not an independent private condemnor).
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
